tldr: Headlines across all 16 outlet clusters frame Andy Burnham's Makerfield by-election win as the opening move of a Labour leadership showdown, casting the result as a direct challenge to Prime Minister Keir Starmer's political survival. The vocabulary of 'oust,' 'topple,' and 'showdown' is near-universal; no outlet treats the result as routine electoral news.

evidence.quote: setting up a showdown with Starmer to lead Britain
Burnham by-election victory raises stakes for Starmer
setting up showdown with Keir Starmer to lead Britain
paves way for bid to oust UK PM Starmer
Starmer 'will stand' in Labour leader battle
How Andy Burnham could attempt to topple Keir Starmer
Rebel Lawmaker Set to Try a Knockout Blow
Clearing Way for Starmer Leadership Challenge
setting up a showdown with Starmer to lead Britain
Burnham wins: So how do they oust Starmer?
'King of the North' Burnham wins seat
seismic UK by-election that imperils PM Starmer
Burnham wins key UK poll, clearing way for Starmer challenge
setting up showdown with Starmer to lead the UK
byelection win 'last chance' to change Britain
what this says about Britain's changing political system

single_outlet_findings.finding: 'Rebel Lawmaker Set to Try a Knockout Blow to U.K.'s Starmer' uniquely applies physical-combat metaphor ('Knockout Blow') to what the rest of the corpus describes as a leadership challenge or showdown. No other title uses boxing or assault-register language.

'What does Burnham's potential by-election win mean for the UK?' treats the outcome as uncertain ('potential'), indicating publication before the result was declared — the only headline in the cluster framing the story prospectively rather than reporting the win.

bottom_line: The headline layer delivers a uniform leadership-battle narrative: Burnham as insurgent challenger, Starmer as embattled incumbent fighting for survival — the result is universally read as the start of a Labour succession contest, not merely an electoral milestone.
